race.name: L'Etape Norway by Tour de France
race.display_name: L'Etape Norway by Tour de France
race.tagline: Norway's inaugural 130km Tour de France experience through Arctic fjords and Nordic climbing

vitals.registration: L'Étape Series format with pre-race packet pickup; includes timing chip, route support, aid stations, and commemorative finisher jersey
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple aid stations along course; typical L'Étape Series support infrastructure
vitals.cutoff_time: Standard L'Étape cutoff times apply (typically 6-7 hours)
route_options: 130 km (80.8 mi), 1,600m elevation — full course through Norwegian terrain

climate.primary: Cool Nordic summer — mild but variable
climate.description: Mid-June in central Norway brings long daylight hours (nearly 24-hour daylight) and mild temperatures between 10-20°C (50-68°F). Expect occasional rain and changeable conditions typical of Scandinavian weather. The extended daylight is a unique advantage for pacing and morale.

terrain.primary: Rolling to mountainous with fjord-influenced landscape
terrain.surface: Mix of paved roads with likely gravel or rough-surface sections (similar to Arctic Race of Norway format). Roads follow Norwegian coastal and valley routes with frequent elevation changes and technical descents. Expect well-maintained primary roads with occasional rougher sections.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 71. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 70.

course_description.character: L'Étape Norway weaves through central Norwegian terrain, combining coastal fjord approaches with inland mountain climbing. The course likely mirrors Arctic Race of Norway's philosophy—mixing technical descents, gravel sections, and sustained climbs across varied terrain. Riders experience the psychological advantage of extended daylight while navigating rolling to mountainous topography. The route balances accessibility for serious amateurs with enough technical and climbing challenges to create meaningful selection. Expect a course that showcases Norwegian cycling culture while delivering Tour de France-caliber route design.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Målselv climb stands as the course's signature challenge—a 3-4km alpine ascent at 7-8% that creates genuine selection while remaining achievable for trained amateurs. This climb defines the race's character and determines the final general classification. The exposed Nordic terrain and potential wind make it tactically interesting beyond pure climbing power.

logistics.airport: Trondheim Airport (TRD) is the primary gateway, offering direct flights from major European hubs. The airport is 30km northeast of the city with bus and rental car options. Expect 1-2 hour transfer time to central Trondheim accommodations.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Book accommodations 3-4 months in advance; Trondheim fills quickly during summer season. Mid-range hotels and guesthouses cluster near the city center. Consider staying in nearby towns (Sørreisa, Harstad) if Trondheim is fully booked, though this adds transfer complexity.
logistics.food: Norway's food costs are steep; budget $25-40 per meal at restaurants. Grocery stores (Coop, Rema 1000) offer better value for self-catering. Pre-race carb-loading at restaurants is expensive; consider bringing familiar nutrition from home.

history.origin_story: L'Étape Norway represents the Tour de France's strategic expansion of its iconic L'Étape Series cyclosportive beyond France into international markets. The original L'Étape du Tour de France launched in 1993, allowing amateurs to ride Tour-inspired routes. By 2025, the L'Étape Series had grown to a global platform attracting 60,000 cyclists across multiple continents. The Trondheim edition leverages the established Arctic Race of Norway (a professional UCI race since 2013) to bring Tour de France prestige to Scandinavia. This inaugural 2026 edition positions Trondheim as a flagship international L'Étape location, capitalizing on Norway's cycling culture and dramatic Nordic scenery.
notable_moments: First L'Étape Series event in Scandinavia (2026)
Partnership with Arctic Race of Norway professional circuit
Inaugural edition during near-24-hour daylight season
Part of L'Étape Series' post-2025 global expansion
history.reputation: L'Étape Series has established itself as the gold standard for amateur cyclosportives, combining Tour de France heritage with professional logistics. The Trondheim edition inherits this reputation while benefiting from Arctic Race of Norway's expertise in northern European cycling. First-edition status carries both prestige and execution uncertainty.

biased_opinion.summary: L'Étape Norway represents the Tour de France brand's successful expansion into Scandinavia, delivering a prestigious inaugural event in one of Europe's most dramatic cycling regions. The 130km course through Trondheim and surrounding fjord terrain offers genuine climbing, technical interest, and the psychological boost of nearly 24-hour daylight. This is not a bargain event—Norway's premium pricing and the international travel required make it expensive—but the combination of Tour de France pedigree, Nordic scenery, and first-edition exclusivity justifies the investment for serious amateur cyclists seeking a memorable experience beyond typical European cyclosportives. The course design leverages Arctic Race of Norway expertise, promising quality route planning and professional support infrastructure. This event appeals to cyclists who value prestige, adventure, and unique geography over cost efficiency.
weaknesses: smaller field than flagship L'Étape events
limited historical data for course difficulty

final_verdict.one_liner: A prestigious Nordic debut that trades cost efficiency for adventure and Tour de France cachet.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you value prestige, Nordic scenery, and inaugural event status enough to justify premium travel costs and unpredictable weather. Skip if you're budget-conscious, prefer proven multi-year events, or want guaranteed warm conditions.
final_verdict.alternatives: L'Étape du Tour de France (French Alps, established prestige), Arctic Race of Norway (professional racing spectacle in same region), Étape Trondheim (same location, different format)

citations.label: L'Etape Trondheim by Tour de France
3 L'Étape Series events to prepare for L'Étape du Tour de France
Arctic Race of Norway 2026 Course Details
Arctic Race of Norway 2025 Course Profile

videos.title: 🇲🇽 L’Étape La Paz by Tour de France 🔥 🚴‍♂️
videos.channel: L'Etape Series by Tour de France
videos.description:
🇲🇽 L’Étape México La Paz delivered once again!

We are still buzzing from the energy at this year’s event. From the golden desert trails to the breathtaking shores of the Sea of Cortez, hundreds of cyclists pushed their limits across the different routes.

A huge thank you to the riders and the volunteers for making this "epic edition" a reality. You brought the passion of the Tour de France to the heart of Baja California Sur! 🌵🚴‍♂️

---------

L'Etape Series is a series of amateur cycling events designed to experience the Tour de France Legend. It is the only event where you can race and feel like a Tour de France champion, and where you can enjoy the Tour de France unique atmosphere and passion.

“The Tour de France is the world's greatest cycling race. Each year it is passionately followed by fans around the world so it has naturally given rise to L'Etape by Tour de France series”, says Christian Prudhomme, Tour de France director. 

The series has now expanded to more than 22 international locations including:

🇦🇷 Argentina
🇧🇷 Brazil
🇧🇬 Bulgaria
🇨🇱 Chile
🇨🇳 China
🇨🇷 Costa Rica
🇨🇿 Czech Republic
🇩🇰 Denmark
🇫🇷 France
🇬🇷 Greece
🇮🇳 India
🇮🇪 Ireland
🇮🇹 Italy
🇲🇽 Mexico
🇳🇴 Norway
🇵🇾 Paraguay
🇵🇱 Poland
🇷🇴 Romania
🇸🇰 Slovakia
🇸🇮 Slovenia

Among these Tour de France official cycling events is of course L'Étape du Tour. With a peloton of 45 000 riders and 50 nationalities, it is Europe's biggest cycling event. Riding on the same routes and under the same conditions as Tour de France champions: this is the promise that L'Étape du Tour has kept since 1993.

Check out https://www.letapeseries.com for more information.

Join us on:
-Facebook 👉 https://www.facebook.com/letapeseries
-Instagram 👉 https://www.instagram.com/letapeseries
-Tiktok 👉 https://www.tiktok.com/@letapeseries
-Strava 👉 https://www.strava.com/clubs/145034

videos.title: How I Finished TOP 2% at L’Étape du Tour 2025
videos.description:
What does it take to finish in the top 2% at one of Europe's hardest Gran Fondos? I rode the infamous L'Etape du Tour 2025 from Albertville to La Plagne - 130 kilometers with over 4300 meters of climbing - to find out.

Check out the Insta360 X5 (affiliate link): https://www.insta360.com/sal/x5?utm_term=INR3YPN
Buy my custom CUORE cycling kit: https://teamshop.cuore.ch/tristan-cardew-collection

See the ride on Strava: https://www.strava.com/activities/15174620066
Become a channel member: https://www.youtube.com/@tristantakevideo
Follow me on Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/tristantakephoto

SUPPORTED BY:
@ride_bmc | @insta360 | @COROSGlobal | @bontcycling | @pelotancc 

DISCOUNTS & AFFILIATE LINKS:
Custom Cuore cycling kit: https://eu.cuore.ch
Filmed with Insta360: https://www.insta360.com/sal/x5?utm_term=INR3YPN
Coros DURA cycling computer: https://noxl.ink/IDn6Lp
20% off your next FUEL+ nutrition order, use code TRISTAN20: https://www.fuelplus.es

00:00 Intro
01:26 About the event 
04:20 Start of the race
07:05 Second climb complete
10:20 Insta360 X5
11:55 Last climb
16:11 Final result & thoughts

This ride was filmed in the French Alps, riding the Tour de France stage 19 of 2025 from Albertville to La Plagne, won by Thymen Arensman, followed closely by Jonas Vingegaard and Tadej Pogacar. We rode over climbs such as the Col du Pre, Cormet de Roselend and Col de Saisies, premiere cycling holiday destinations.

videos.title: 2018 Tour de France - Etape 17 Recon - Peyragudes / Azet / Col de Portet (Édition Française)
videos.description:
Don't need French subtitles? Watch our English version here https://youtu.be/y_3b3_tIDaM

L’attente de la présentation du parcours du Tour de France est toujours une période excitante. Des semaines de devinettes à rêver à quels cols seront gravis, tout se concrétise finalement une fois toutes les cartes mises sur table. Au fur et à mesure que le parcours se dévoile, étape après étape, nous apprenons enfin que la course se dirigera vers les Pyrénées pour sa dernière semaine, ce qui semble être l'une des meilleures façon de terminer le Tour! Maintenant, cela ne surprendra peut-être personne, mais ce qui a attiré notre attention avant tout (et celle du reste du monde du cyclisme), c'est l'étape 17 hyper dynamique, qui commence à Bagnères-de-Luchon avec au menu la montée de Peyragudes, le Col de Val Louron-Azet et le tout nouveau Col de Portet. Trois montées successives avec plus de 3000 mètres d’ascension en seulement 65 kilomètres! Seulement 65 km du début à la fin ...... journée folle!

Je serai honnête, je n’ai jamais de problème à trouver la motivation pour une longue journée en montagne même quand l’automne s'empare rapidement des montagnes avec une première couche de neige sur les sommets les plus élevés. Mais avec un parcours aussi alléchant si près de chez moi, je ne pouvais plus m’endormir jusqu'à ce que j’expérimente ce tracé moi-même, d'autant plus que ce sera la première fois qu’une course utilisera le Col de Portet, et à 2215 mètres de hauteur, il dépasse le légendaire Col du Tourmalet de 100 mètres. Quelle fin d’étape!

Ayant roulé et couru pendant plus de 25 ans, c’est ce type d'étapes qui m'excite vraiment. Court, explosif dès le départ, nulle part où se cacher. Que les montagnes à conquérir et un million de spectateurs qui bordent la route du début à la fin. Qu'est-ce qui pourrait être mieux? Pour ma part, j'espère que c'est le début d'une nouvelle ère avec plus d’étapes de ce genre dans les Grand Tours.

Départ: Bagnères-de-Luchon
Distance: 65km
Point culminant: 2215m
Ascension totale: 3126m
Pente maximale: 13%

J'espère que vous apprécierez notre reconnaissance du parcours. La beauté de cette étape va au-delà des mots, certainement l'une des meilleures que j'ai eu l'honneur de parcourir.

Jusqu’à ce que les professionnels lui donnent vie en juillet prochain, roulez prudemment et profitez-en!

Mike Cotty
The Col Collective

Ride with The Col Collective: http://thecolcollective.com/cycling-tours
Subscribe on YouTube: http://www.youtube.com/user/TheColCollective?sub_confirmation=1
Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/TheColCollective 
Twitter: https://twitter.com/colcollective
Instagram: http://instagram.com/thecolcollective
Google+: https://plus.google.com/+TheColCollective

videos.title: EV 15 Rhine Cycle Route |  Amsterdam to Millingen | North Sea to Adriatic Cycle Tour | Ep 1
videos.description:
This is the first stage of our 2022 ride from Amsterdam in the Netherlands to Grado, Italy on a route that includes a short section of EuroVelo 12, EuroVelo 15 (the Rhine route), EuroVelo 6 along the Danube, part of the Tauern Radweg and finally the Cyclovia Alpe Adria Radweg (Alps to Adriatic). Our ride began on May 6th and we reached the Adriatic Sea on June 30th having clocked up over 2800 km.

This first episode takes us from the Netherlands coast to the German border, with lots of drama on Day 2! As you will see, not everything went to plan, but it's all part of the adventure.


Useful Resources:
For EuroVelo 15 we used the Cicerone book "The Rhine Cycle Route" by Mike Wells and found it very useful. It has detailed maps and interesting background information too. The maps were particularly useful when the trail signage wasn't enough. I have used some of these maps in this video series.

The official EuroVelo website is another handy resource of course, and for those using GPS navigation, it provides gpx files for the various routes. https://en.eurovelo.com/#routes-and-countries

For navigation, we did not use any high-tech GPS systems, relying instead on route signage, the book "The Rhine Cycle Route" (Kindle version) and a useful free overview map of EV 15 that I picked up in a tourist office. For other route-finding to campgrounds, grocery shops, etc, we used the Maps.Me phone application which you can use offline.
We did not have local phone sim cards so our use of Google maps and other online resources was limited to when we had wifi access at campgrounds, grocery shops and restaurants.

All music in this episode is from Epidemic Sound.

0:00 Intro and Route overview
2:30 Journey begins in Amsterdam
4:20 Day 1 Amsterdam to the coast via Haarlem
8:20 Day 2 Hague to Hook of Holland, broken bike drama!
14:40 Hanging out at the Hook during bike repair
16:07 Ride resumes, Day 3 - Rotterdam, windmills, the River Lek and farm camping
22:17 Day 4 Schoonhoven to Wijk bij Duurstede
26:07 Day 5 Wijk bij D to Wageningen, bike repaired, some WW2 history
30:04 Wageningen to Millingen, reach German border

key_challenges.name: The final climb to Alpe d'Huez
key_challenges.description: A massive 20 km long climb averaging around 7% grade that ends the race

gear_mentions.text: Smaller chainrings recommended due to the amount of climbing - there are five climbs on the course

race_day_tips.text: Use the 19 km descent after the first climb as time to eat, drink, and recover
Maintain an easy 6 watts per kilo pace on the early climbs

additional_quotes.text: I'm actually number 17,500. So, I thought I was starting right at the back of the event, but it turns out they put a little number zero in the corner of my race number there, which means I'm actually in wave zero.

rider_intel.search_text: L'Etape Norway by Tour de France is a 135km gran fondo with 4,500 meters of elevation gain featuring five major climbs. The race attracts approximately 15,000 participants, making it one of Europe's largest fondos. The course replicates an actual Tour de France stage route. Key challenges include a massive final climb to Alpe d'Huez that's 20 kilometers long averaging 7% grade. Riders recommend using smaller chainrings due to the extensive climbing. The race features wave starts with numbered categories. There's a significant 19km descent after the first climb that provides opportunity for nutrition and recovery. Pacing strategy involves maintaining an easy 6 watts per kilo on early climbs. The event takes place in July in the French Alps, requiring pre-race registration and offering a full event village experience for participants.
